summary:
The February Revolution of 1917 was the first of two revolutions that year in Russia, resulting in the abdication of Tsar Nicholas II and the end of the Romanov dynasty after three centuries of rule. The revolution began with bread riots and strikes in Petrograd (St. Petersburg) on March 8 (February 23 by the old Julian calendar), which rapidly escalated when army units refused to fire on protesters and instead joined the uprising. Within days the tsar's government had lost control of the capital.

Nicholas II abdicated on March 15 in favor of a Provisional Government drawn from members of the State Duma, with Prince Georgy Lvov as head of government and Alexander Kerensky as a prominent minister. Simultaneously, soviets (workers' and soldiers' councils) sprang up across Russia, creating a situation of dual power in which the Provisional Government lacked the authority to enforce its decisions without the cooperation of the Petrograd Soviet.

The Provisional Government made the fateful decision to continue the war, which proved deeply unpopular with war-exhausted soldiers and workers. This decision created the political opening that the Bolsheviks under Vladimir Lenin, who returned to Russia in April after years of exile, exploited with their demand for immediate peace, land redistribution, and soviet power. The tension between the Provisional Government and the soviets culminated in the [[event:2646|October Revolution]] eight months later.
